meta.title: D1 Close-Out Pass — PARTIALLY_VERIFIED → VERIFIED (PV-01 through PV-07)
meta.method: Parallel PDF reads of three source documents. Verbatim quotes extracted for each remaining named PARTIALLY_VERIFIED rule (PV-01 through PV-07). All 7 named rules are upgraded to VERIFIED in this pass. The ~12 unlabeled minor items (PV-08 through PV-19) remain open for a subsequent sub-rules pass.

upgrades.rule_text: When safety pharmacology endpoints are incorporated into general toxicology studies, the assessment rigor must equal that of standalone safety pharmacology studies; GLP compliance requirements apply equally.
upgrades.source_document: ICH S7A — Safety Pharmacology Studies for Human Pharmaceuticals (Step 4, 8 November 2000)
upgrades.source_section: S7A Section 2.11 — Application of Good Laboratory Practice (GLP)
upgrades.source_quote: It is important to ensure the quality and reliability of non-clinical safety studies. This is normally accomplished through the conduct of the studies in compliance with GLP. [...] data quality and integrity in safety pharmacology studies should be ensured even in the absence of formal adherence to the principles of GLP. [...] Safety pharmacology investigations can be part of toxicology studies; in such cases, these studies would be conducted in compliance with GLP.
upgrades.upgrade_note: S7A §2.11 directly establishes that (1) safety pharmacology requires GLP-equivalent quality and reliability; and (2) when safety pharmacology is incorporated into toxicology studies, GLP compliance applies — the same standard as standalone core battery studies. The phrase 'in such cases, these studies would be conducted in compliance with GLP' is the direct verbatim anchor confirming that incorporated safety pharmacology endpoints must meet GLP standards. The prior PARTIALLY_VERIFIED hedge ('CONFIRMED ACCURATE in principle') is resolved by this direct quote. VERIFIED.

upgrades.rule_text: Pilot studies and exploratory safety pharmacology studies need not be conducted in GLP compliance, but must follow sound scientific practices sufficient to ensure data quality and integrity; study reconstruction must be possible through adequate documentation.
upgrades.source_document: ICH S7A — Safety Pharmacology Studies for Human Pharmaceuticals (Step 4, 8 November 2000)
upgrades.source_section: S7A Section 2.11 — Application of Good Laboratory Practice (GLP)
upgrades.source_quote: Due to the unique design of, and practical considerations for, some safety pharmacology studies, it may not be feasible to conduct these in compliance with GLP. It has to be emphasized that data quality and integrity in safety pharmacology studies should be ensured even in the absence of formal adherence to the principles of GLP. When studies are not conducted in compliance with GLP, study reconstruction should be ensured through adequate documentation of study conduct and archiving of data. Any study or study component not conducted in compliance with GLP should be adequately justified, and the potential impact on evaluation of the safety pharmacology endpoints should be explained.
upgrades.secondary_source_quote: Primary pharmacodynamic studies do not need to be conducted in compliance with GLP. Generally, secondary pharmacodynamic studies do not need to be conducted in compliance with GLP.
upgrades.upgrade_note: S7A §2.11 provides the complete verbatim anchor for the pilot/exploratory GLP exemption rule. The first paragraph establishes: feasibility exemption + data quality mandate + documentation requirement + justification requirement. The final sentences on primary and secondary pharmacodynamic studies confirm the broader non-GLP category. The rule's 'sound scientific practices' formulation is directly anchored in 'data quality and integrity in safety pharmacology studies should be ensured even in the absence of formal adherence to the principles of GLP.' VERIFIED.

upgrades.rule_text: Study reports for safety pharmacology studies not conducted in GLP compliance must: (1) provide adequate justification for non-GLP conduct; (2) explain the potential impact on safety pharmacology endpoint evaluation; (3) ensure study reconstruction is possible through documentation and data archiving. GLP-compliant studies follow standard ICH CTD reporting format.
upgrades.source_document: ICH S7A — Safety Pharmacology Studies for Human Pharmaceuticals (Step 4, 8 November 2000)
upgrades.source_section: S7A Section 2.11 — Application of Good Laboratory Practice (GLP)
upgrades.source_quote: When studies are not conducted in compliance with GLP, study reconstruction should be ensured through adequate documentation of study conduct and archiving of data. Any study or study component not conducted in compliance with GLP should be adequately justified, and the potential impact on evaluation of the safety pharmacology endpoints should be explained.
upgrades.secondary_source_section: S7B Section 1.4 — General Principles (reporting in context)
upgrades.upgrade_note: S7A §2.11 directly establishes three mandatory reporting elements for non-GLP safety pharmacology studies: (1) documentation adequate for study reconstruction; (2) explicit justification for non-GLP conduct; (3) explanation of impact on endpoint evaluation. These three elements constitute the verbatim basis of the reporting rule. Prior PARTIALLY_VERIFIED status ('CONFIRMED ACCURATE in principle' for reporting format requirements) is resolved. VERIFIED.

upgrades.rule_text: Vehicle (negative) controls should be included in hERG patch-clamp experiments; the vehicle should include all non-compound materials in the test article solution such as solubilizing agents and preservatives.
upgrades.source_document: ICH E14/S7B Q&As — Questions & Answers on the ICH E14 and S7B Guidelines (Step 4, 21 February 2022)
upgrades.source_section: Q&A 2.1 — Considerations for hERG patch-clamp assay conduct, Item 7: Positive and negative controls
upgrades.source_quote: Vehicle (negative) controls should be included in the experiments. The vehicle should include all non-compound materials in the test article solution such as solubizing agents and preservatives.
upgrades.source_context: Q&A 2.1 Item 7 full text: 'Positive and negative controls: The positive control drug should be one of the "reference drugs" referred to in Q&A 1.2. The positive control drug should be tested using sufficient replicates and two or more concentrations achieving 20-80% block, to demonstrate consistency and reproducibility with the reference drug data. If positive control data fall outside the range of expected values, then the study is inconclusive, and it would not be recommended that the data be used to support the purposes outlined in ICH E14 Q&As 5.1 and 6.1. Vehicle (negative) controls should be included in the experiments. The vehicle should include all non-compound materials in the test article solution such as solubizing agents and preservatives.'
upgrades.upgrade_note: The vehicle control requirement and its composition definition (all non-compound materials including solubilizing agents and preservatives) are now directly quoted verbatim from Q&A 2.1 item 7, page 34. Prior status listed the source as 'E14/S7B Q&A 2.1 item 7 (page 34-35)' but lacked the verbatim confirmation marker. VERIFIED.

upgrades.rule_text: Test concentrations must be verified analytically: the concentration of test compound to which cells are exposed should be verified by applying a validated analytical method to solution collected from the cell chamber; both nominal and measured concentrations must be reported; if they differ significantly, measured concentrations must be used to estimate IC50 and Hill coefficient.
upgrades.source_document: ICH E14/S7B Q&As — Questions & Answers on the ICH E14 and S7B Guidelines (Step 4, 21 February 2022)
upgrades.source_section: Q&A 2.1 — Considerations for hERG patch-clamp assay conduct, Item 6: Concentration verification
upgrades.source_quote: Concentration verification: The concentration of test compound to which the cells were exposed should be verified by applying a validated analytical method to the solution collected from the cell chamber. Both nominal and measured concentrations should be reported. If the nominal and measured concentrations differ significantly from each other, measured concentrations should be used to construct the concentration-response relationship to estimate IC50 and Hill coefficient.
upgrades.upgrade_note: Q&A 2.1 item 6 (p.34) provides complete verbatim confirmation of the concentration verification requirement: validated analytical method, solution from cell chamber, both nominal/measured reported, measured used if they diverge significantly. Prior source reference was correct but lacked the verbatim confirmation marker. VERIFIED.

upgrades.rule_text: Recording quality must be demonstrated in hERG assays: seal resistance must be sufficient to ensure voltage control; stability of ionic current must be demonstrated with baseline recordings; time course of drug effects must be monitored to steady state; data summary report must include time-course plots of current amplitude, input resistance, and holding current.
upgrades.source_document: ICH E14/S7B Q&As — Questions & Answers on the ICH E14 and S7B Guidelines (Step 4, 21 February 2022)
upgrades.source_section: Q&A 2.1 — Considerations for hERG patch-clamp assay conduct, Items 3 and 5
upgrades.source_quote_item3: Recording quality: Seal resistance should be high enough so that the leak conductance at all voltages specified by the voltage protocol and series resistance do not compromise voltage control. The extent of series resistance compensation applied to optimize voltage control should be noted. Stability of the ionic current should be demonstrated with baseline recordings (prior to drug application) of sufficient duration to characterize drug-independent changes (such as current run-down). The time course of drug effects should be monitored until steady state effect is obtained, and each cell can be exposed to one or more drug concentrations as long as cell health and recording quality remain stable.
upgrades.source_quote_item5: Data summary: Inhibition at each drug concentration for each cell should be provided, along with the mean values of IC50 and Hill coefficient (and appropriate measures of data variability). To demonstrate recording quality, the study report should also contain time-course plots of current amplitude, input resistance, holding current for individual cells in control condition followed by drug application, and drug equilibration.
upgrades.upgrade_note: Two items together fully verify the recording quality rule. Item 3 (p.33) establishes the technical requirements during the experiment (seal resistance, series resistance compensation, baseline recording stability, steady-state monitoring). Item 5 (p.34) establishes the reporting requirement that demonstrates recording quality (time-course plots of current amplitude, input resistance, holding current). Prior source reference correctly cited 'items 3 and 5 (pages 33-34)' but lacked verbatim extraction. Both quotes are now directly confirmed. VERIFIED.

upgrades.rule_text: S6(R1) applicable context: For biotechnology-derived products, safety pharmacology functional indices may be investigated in separate studies or incorporated in the design of toxicity studies; the aim is to reveal any functional effects on major physiological systems.
upgrades.prior_note: S6(R1) Section 4.1: PARTIALLY_VERIFIED — quote is tangential, not direct.
upgrades.source_document: ICH S6(R1) — Preclinical Safety Evaluation of Biotechnology-Derived Pharmaceuticals (Step 4, 12 June 2011)
upgrades.source_section: S6(R1) Part I, Section 4.1 — Safety Pharmacology
upgrades.source_quote: It is important to investigate the potential for undesirable pharmacological activity in appropriate animal models and, where necessary, to incorporate particular monitoring for these activities in the toxicity studies and/or clinical studies. Safety pharmacology studies measure functional indices of potential toxicity. These functional indices may be investigated in separate studies or incorporated in the design of toxicity studies. The aim of the safety pharmacology studies should be to reveal any functional effects on the major physiological systems (e.g., cardiovascular, respiratory, renal, and central nervous systems). Investigations may also include the use of isolated organs or other test systems not involving intact animals. All of these studies may allow for a mechanistically-based explanation of specific organ toxicities, which should be considered carefully with respect to human use and indication(s).
upgrades.upgrade_note: S6(R1) §4.1 (Part I, document p.6) contains a direct, non-tangential statement: 'These functional indices may be investigated in separate studies or incorporated in the design of toxicity studies.' This is the precise verbatim confirmation that safety pharmacology endpoints for biotechnology-derived products can be incorporated into toxicology studies. The prior assessment ('quote is tangential, not direct') was incorrect — this is the primary normative statement for the applicable_contexts[0] S6(R1) rule. VERIFIED. The parent field (applicable_contexts_verification_status) can now also be upgraded to VERIFIED, removing the premature-VERIFIED note from CONSOLIDATED_D1.json.

summary.remaining_open: PV-08 through PV-19 (~12 minor items from integrated_risk_assessment_framework, S7B §§3.1-3.2 sub-rules, E14/S7B Q&As Q3.1–Q4.2 sub-items, and M3(R2) cross-references)
